content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Delaying academic selection beyond age 12 in Hungary and extending common schooling to age 16, bundled with university-based teacher-education reform and protected against school-choice-driven substitution of formal tracking with within-school segregation, would be expected to reduce achievement gaps and raise intergenerational mobility, but only if the full policy package is implemented coherently.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Finland's 1972–1977 comprehensive school reform delayed tracking from age 11 to age 16, eliminating early selection into vocational and general tracks.
Delaying tracking to age 16 reduced intergenerational income elasticity by 23%, from 0.30 to 0.23, indicating substantial gains in social mobility.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: The causal contribution of tracking-age delay alone versus the full Finnish policy bundle (welfare expansion, teacher-education upgrade, curricular change) remains uncertain; isolating the tracking effect requires counterfactual scenarios not available from Finnish data.
